display_name: A single log message does not prove causation — look for patterns and correlation
context: Reviewing device logs during an incident and finding a syslog message that appears related
observation: A syslog message or event log entry appears around the same time as the reported issue
naive_interpretation: This log message is the cause of (or directly related to) the problem being investigated
correct_interpretation: Temporal proximity does not equal causation. Network devices generate many log messages continuously. Finding one near the incident time proves nothing by itself. Establish causation by checking: does the message repeat in a pattern that correlates with symptom onset/offset? Does the message indicate a condition that would mechanistically produce the observed symptoms? Have you ruled out that the message is routine background noise?
why: Network devices can generate hundreds of log messages per minute during normal operation
Human pattern matching is biased toward finding connections — seeing a log near an incident time feels causal even when it is coincidental
Many log messages are informational or relate to routine operations (OSPF hello processing, NTP sync, SNMP polls) that have nothing to do with the issue
The actual root cause may not generate a log message at all, or the log may appear earlier than expected due to buffering

confirmation_commands.purpose: Review broader log context around the incident — look for clusters of related messages, not single entries
Check how frequently the suspect message appears — if it occurs every 5 minutes normally, its presence during the incident is meaningless
Check message frequency and pattern to distinguish signal from noise

traps: Fixating on the first interesting-looking log message near the incident timestamp
Not checking whether the same log message appears regularly during normal operation
Assuming the log timestamp exactly matches when the event occurred — syslog can be delayed by buffering or rate-limiting
Treating a log message as root cause without a mechanistic explanation of how that event produces the observed symptoms
